# HockeyViz Magnus Expected Goals Model

> **Source:** https://hockeyviz.com/txt/magnusEV , https://hockeyviz.com/txt/fabricxg , https://hockeyviz.com/txt/xg4
> **Author:** Micah Blake McCurdy
> **Retrieved:** 2026-04-08
> **Type:** Public xG model documentation
> **Note:** Direct page fetches returned 403; content compiled from search result excerpts and cached descriptions.

## Algorithm

Regularized logistic regression. The observation vector is 1 for goals, 0 for saves/misses. Model form: Y ~ l(Xb), where l is the logistic function, X is the design matrix, and b is the coefficient vector.

## Three-Fold xG Decomposition

Magnus separates goal probability into three components:

1. **Base xG** — the goal likelihood of the shot as generated by all skaters (location, type, context)
2. **Shooting talent** — individual shooter finishing impact, modifying base xG
3. **Goaltending** — individual goalie save ability, modifying base xG

xG represents the combined ability of all skaters leading up to and including the decision to shoot. Shooter and goalie effects are treated as modifiers on this base probability.

## Spatial Fabric (Hex Grid)

Shot locations encoded via a hexagonal grid covering the offensive zone:
- Each hex corresponds to a model covariate
- One hex for shots from below the goal line
- One "out of zone" hex for shots beyond the blue line
- Fabric coarseness chosen to match accuracy of NHL location recording and produce smooth estimates
- Each hex has a constant term providing "starting odds" for shots from that location

## Features (as model covariates)

- **Hex location** — spatial starting odds (the primary geometric term)
- **Shot type indicators** — covariates modifying the hex odds
- **Rebound indicator** — odds ratio ~2x (rebounds approximately double goal odds)
- **Rush indicator** — odds ratio ~2x (rush chances approximately double goal odds)
- **Score state** — trailing teams shoot worse than tied teams; leading teams show negligible change
- **Venue** — home/away indicator
- **Fatigue / time** — contextual time factors
- **Shooter indicators** — per-player coefficients for finishing talent
- **Goaltender indicators** — per-player coefficients for save ability

All non-geometric covariates are interpreted as odds ratios modifying the base hex probability.

## Regularization

- **Ridge penalty** (L2 regularization / zero-biased regression)
- Earlier versions (Magnus 1-2): single ridge penalty toward zero
- Later versions: penalty toward previous season's estimate
- These represent conceptually different priors — one about the distribution (population shrinkage) and one about individuals (temporal smoothing)

Historical context: first hockey application of regularized regression was Brian MacDonald (2012), followed by Schuckers & Curro (THoR), then Emmanuel Perry (Corsica) and the Younggrens (Evolving Hockey).

## Model Versions

Magnus has been iteratively refined through at least 9 versions:
- Magnus 3: introduced spatial fabric and xG/shooting/goaltending decomposition
- Magnus 4: refined xG, shooting, and goaltending separation
- Magnus 5: added special teams (ST) model
- Magnus 6-9: continued refinements (specific changes per version documented at hockeyviz.com)

Even-strength (EV) and special-teams (ST) are modeled separately.

## Design Matrix Construction

The design matrix X encodes each shot with:
- Indicators for the hex location
- Indicators for shot type
- Indicators for shooter identity
- Indicators for goaltender identity
- Contextual covariates (score, venue, fatigue, rush, rebound)

The logistic regression then estimates coefficients that can be interpreted as log-odds ratios.

## Key Analytical Insights

- Score effects: trailing teams shoot worse (lower xG per shot) than tied teams; leading teams show negligible change in shot quality
- Rush and rebound shots have ~2x odds ratios, substantially improving goal likelihood
- The hex fabric approach implicitly handles distance and angle through spatial location rather than explicit distance/angle features
